exemplar.title: To what extent does parental divorce impact the Achievements of Students 17 years old and under, and the correlation to attachment theory

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does parental divorce impact the Achievements of Students 17 years old and under, and the correlation to attachment theory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic and theoretical lens are communicated clearly and consistently throughout.
Research question is explicitly stated, focused and sustained across all sections.
weaknesses: Methodology description lacks detail on search strategy, screening criteria and source selection procedures.
Minor stylistic inconsistencies in restating the research question reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the topic and research question are communicated accurately (band 5â6), the methodology is only partially developed (band 3â4). The weighted average of the three strands (5, 5 and 3) falls solidly into the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Demonstrates good subject knowledge with relevant classic and recent research (e.g., Bowlby, Ainsworth, Osman & Bulut).
Terminology such as secure/insecure attachment and dependent/independent variables is generally accurate and appropriately applied.
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formatting and occasional missing publication details reduce academic rigor.
Some terminological slips (e.g., mislabeling hypotheses) interrupt conceptual precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Source application and understanding are consistently clear (band 3â4), and subject-specific terminology is mostly accurate (band 3â4). The combination of both strands at the top of the 3â4 band supports a score of 4.

strengths: Bibliography shows broad, relevant reading across longitudinal studies and meta-analyses.
Argument structure includes supporting and counterarguments, and concludes with coherent suggestions for future research.
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is uneven; quantitative details (effect sizes, confidence intervals) are rarely examined.
Evaluation of limitations and confounders remains largely descriptive rather than critically reflective.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality is strong and mostly relevant (band 7â9), while analysis and evaluation meet the 4â6 band descriptors. The overall balance places the work in the mid-range of the 4â6 band, justifying a score of 6.

strengths: Report follows EE conventions with clear structure, table of contents, numbered sections and consistent citation style.
Headings such as âA. Supporting Argumentâ aid readability and navigation.
weaknesses: Occasional spacing and line-break errors disrupt flow.
Some sections lack clear signposting or subheadings, causing minor reader disorientation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both presentation strands lie in the 3â4 band: structure is appropriate and formatting conventions are met, with only minor errors. This supports a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological termsâsecure/insecure attachment, null/alternative hypothesis, independent/dependent variablesâare used and usually defined correctly.  Nevertheless, occasional inaccuracies (labeling H1 as âpositive,â confusing âachievementâ with âaggressionâ) and stylistic inconsistency reduce precision.  Terminological control is mostly accurate rather than consistently so, placing the work in the middle of the 3â4 band.

The title, word count and headings are present; in-text citations and a works-cited list follow a consistent (if imperfect) style.  Page numbers are shown in the table of contents and implied in the extract.  Occasional spacing and line-break errors occur, yet overall layout meets EE formatting conventions, warranting a score in the higher band.

The student summarises a good spread of classic and recent research, correctly cites Bowlby, Ainsworth, Amato, Brand, etc., and recognises methodological nuances (e.g., instrumental-variable design in GrÃ¤tz & HÃ¤rkÃ¶nen).  Source material is generally relevant and supports the narrative, though at times is presented sequentially without deep integration.  Overall knowledge is clear but not consistently sophisticated, meriting the top of the 3â4 band.

The essay operates as a literature review, drawing on a reasonable range of journal articles, meta-analyses and reports.  However, the student offers only a brief, largely descriptive outline of variables and hypotheses; there is no systematic explanation of how sources were located, screened or prioritised, and no discussion of alternative methods.  Consequently the methodology is mostly complete but only partly demonstrates informed selection, matching the 3-4 band.

The student presents a clearly defined topic â the impact of parental divorce on academic achievement through Attachment Theory â and reiterates it coherently in the introduction, discussion and conclusion.  The purpose (to evaluate the strength of the correlation and weigh competing evidence) is explicit and consistently referenced.  Minor stylistic issues (re-stating the RQ with slightly different wording) do not undermine overall clarity; therefore the work fulfils the descriptor of accurate, effective communication with appropriate focus.

The bibliography shows wide reading across peer-reviewed journals, longitudinal datasets and meta-analyses.  Most sources directly address some combination of divorce, attachment and academic outcomes, demonstrating clear relevance.  Minor inclusions (e.g., Schacterâs cognitive-neuroscience article) are tangential, so âconsistentlyâ relevant is not fully met, but overall coverage is solid, fitting the lower end of the 7â9 band.

The research question is explicitly stated twice and remains the thread for source selection and discussion.  It is specific (students â¤17, parental divorce, Attachment Theory) and sufficiently narrow for a 4 000-word EE.  All main sections reference the RQ, demonstrating a sustained focus, although occasional digressions into broader sociocultural issues prevent the fullest level of precision; hence the lower end of the 5â6 band is appropriate.

The essay follows EE conventions: title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, numbered sections, introduction-body-discussion-conclusion, reference list.  Headings and sequencing enable reader navigation; minor issues (duplication of content, uneven section lengths) prevent a full score but the structure is clearly appropriate.

A logical argument structure (supporting vs. counter-arguments) is evident, and the conclusion recognises complexity by partially accepting the null hypothesis.  However, evaluation of study limitations is brief and mainly descriptive; critical reflection on sample bias, cultural generalisability or attachment-theory constraints is limited.  Thus the work meets, but does not exceed, the descriptor for a reasoned argument with partially critical evaluation.

The student compares findings, notes mediating variables (age at divorce, maternal education, pre-divorce conflict) and attempts to weigh evidence in the discussion.  Claims are usually supported with citations, but depth is uneven: statistical details are rarely examined and contrasting methodologies are not critiqued.  Analysis is therefore clear and mostly supported but not consistently effective, matching the middle of the 4â6 band.

annotations.comment: Use of Osman and Bulut (2022) demonstrates awareness of recent meta-analyses and current trends.
Critically appraise sample characteristics and generalizability of preterm child study (Nusinovici et al.) to deepen evaluation.
The counterargument section is located on page 10, but never clearly signposted earlier, causing reader disorientation.
The counterargument acknowledges individual resilience, enriching the analysis with nuanced perspectives.
Summary of Brand et al. study is cut off mid-sentence. Ensure full conclusions are presented for clarity.
Instrumental variable analysis by GrÃ¤tz & HÃ¤rkÃ¶nen is well chosen, showing effective source selection.
Tamelia et al.âs qualitative themes (âdesire,â âself-actionâ) lack clear operational definitions; define themes to support analysis.
The discussion section reiterates findings descriptively; integrate and compare sources to build a more critical evaluation.
Sub-headings in the discussion would improve readability and highlight shifts between supporting and counter perspectives.
The conclusion succinctly summarizes findings and suggests future research, demonstrating coherent understanding.
The title clearly conveys the topic and theoretical lens, meeting the requirement for a report title at the start.
The research question is split across two lines, risking clarity. Combine into one complete sentence for stronger focus.
The word count is correctly stated under 4â000 words, satisfying formatting requirements.
The table of contents provides clear navigation but lacks dot leaders, making alignment uneven. Consider adding dot leaders.
The table of contents follows a logical structure, meeting presentation conventions for easy navigation.
The introduction effectively frames the topic and theoretical lens, clearly communicating purpose and focus.
Citation âCowan et alâ lacks publication year. Use full in-text citations consistently to support academic rigor.
The transition between background and research question is repetitive. Condense to improve coherence and conciseness.
The methodology description does not specify search strategy or inclusion criteria. Detail databases searched, keywords and screening steps.
Hypotheses are mislabeled: H1 should be âalternative hypothesis,â not âpositive.â Use standard statistical terminology.
Variables are clearly defined and controlled (age, dependent variable: performance), demonstrating an understanding of experimental design.
Overview of Bowlby and Ainsworth on page 5 is thorough, showing strong subject knowledge and conceptual grounding.
Page 5 text relies heavily on description without integrating studies to build an argument. Aim for synthesis rather than summary.
Introduce explicit linking sentence after theoretical overview to guide reader to its relevance for academic outcomes.
âA. Supporting Argumentâ heading aids structure and reader orientation, demonstrating clear presentation.
Supporting studies mention GPA differences without detailing effect sizes or confidence intervals. Include quantitative depth.
Consider discussing possible confounders like socioeconomic status or parental education when evaluating supporting evidence.
General statements about emotional distress (page 7) require explicit citations for each claim to strengthen analysis.
The conclusion introduces aggression as a new topic not discussed earlier; remove or integrate earlier to avoid tangents.
The works-cited list mixes formatting styles (italics, math tags). Standardize citation style consistently throughout.
Page numbers appear only in the table of contents. Ensure visible page numbering on each page header or footer.
Reflection on methodological choices and challenges is limited. Add an evaluative reflection on decision-making to enhance engagement.
